mainEntity.name: How much does a Land Rover Range Rover 3.6 TDV8 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned Range Rover 3.6 TDV8 (368DT) engine typically costs £5,500–£9,000 supply only. Fitting adds £1,000–£1,800 at independent specialist rates. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your vehicle.

mainEntity.name: Is the Land Rover Range Rover 3.6 TDV8 engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The 368DT is a fundamentally robust V8 diesel with a CGI block capable of 350,000 km. However, age-related issues with rubber air pipes, plastic thermostat housings, exhaust manifold gaskets, and turbo vane mechanisms are well-documented. Properly maintained, it's a reliable and refined engine; neglected, it becomes very expensive.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Range Rover 3.6 TDV8?

acceptedAnswer.text: Air pipe cracking and boost leaks (the most common issue), exhaust manifold gasket failure (labour-intensive to repair), turbocharger vane mechanism wear (variable geometry turbos), and thermostat housing cracking (can cause sudden coolant loss). All are manageable with preventative maintenance and early detection.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Range Rover 3.6 TDV8 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Against a well-maintained L322 or L320 (values £5,000–£20,000+), a £6,000–£8,000 reconditioned engine is 30–60% of the vehicle's value — often worth it for a solid example. The Land Rover scrap threshold of 60% means evaluating the whole vehicle before committing.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Range Rover 3.6 TDV8 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engine code 368DT is listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The 3.6 TDV8 was fitted to L322 Range Rovers from late 2006 to 2010 and L320 Range Rover Sports from 2007 to 2010. Check your V5C or VIN plate for confirmation.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the turbochargers, or do they require engine replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Turbocharger vane mechanism wear is usually repairable without replacing the engine. A turbo rebuild or replacement costs £1,500–£3,000. However, if a turbo has failed catastrophically and sent debris into the engine, a full engine replacement may be required. At the first sign of boost issues or turbo noise, book an inspection immediately.

replacementCosts.h2: Land Rover Range Rover 3.6 TDV8 Engine Replacement Costs

rows.bestFor: Most owners — best cost/warranty balance for a V8 diesel
Engine no longer produced new; reconditioned units are the standard

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the Range Rover L322 and L320 3.6 TDV8 368DT era. This engine was only fitted 2006–2010, so no newer-generation variance applies.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a 3.6 TDV8 engine typically takes 14–20 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). The L320's tighter engine bay can add labour time. [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet]

cta.label: → Compare Range Rover 3.6 TDV8 engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: Air Pipe Cracking & Boost Leaks
problems.symptoms: Loss of power, hissing noise under acceleration, black smoke, engine warning light, reduced fuel economy.

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon; boost leaks can lead to turbo damage if ignored

problems.recommendation: The 368DT's rubber and plastic air intake pipes are a well-documented weak point. They become brittle with age and heat cycling, cracking and causing boost leaks. This is the single most common issue on these engines. Symptoms include a hissing noise under acceleration, loss of power, and black smoke. Replacement pipes are available and the repair is straightforward, but the parts themselves can be expensive. Proactive inspection of all air pipes at 60,000-mile intervals is recommended.

problems.title: Exhaust Manifold Gasket Failure
problems.symptoms: Ticking or blowing noise from the engine bay (especially on cold start), exhaust smell in cabin, loss of power, reduced fuel economy.
problems.repairCost: £800–£2,000 (gasket replacement — body-off or engine-out on some models) [PLACEHOLDER]
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (unless gasket failure leads to manifold cracking)

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if blowing noise is present

problems.recommendation: The exhaust manifold gaskets on the 368DT are prone to failure. The problem is not the gasket itself — it's the access. On both the L322 and L320, replacing these gaskets often requires significant disassembly. Some specialists report needing to remove the body from the chassis on certain models to access the manifolds properly. If you hear a ticking or blowing noise from the engine bay that increases with engine speed, get it diagnosed immediately. A small leak can be repaired; a large leak can damage the manifold or allow exhaust gases into the cabin.

problems.title: Turbocharger Vane Mechanism Wear (Variable Geometry Turbo)
problems.symptoms: Loss of boost, hesitation on acceleration, overboosting, engine warning light, reduced power, black smoke.
problems.repairCost: £1,500–£3,000 (turbo rebuild or replacement — one or both turbos) [PLACEHOLDER]
problems.replacementCost: £5,000–£9,000 (if turbo failure leads to engine damage) [PLACEHOLDER]

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if boost issues develop

problems.recommendation: The 368DT uses two BorgWarner BV39 variable-geometry turbochargers. The variable vane mechanism can become sticky or fail, leading to boost control issues. This is often caused by carbon buildup from the EGR system or oil degradation. Early symptoms include hesitation on acceleration or overboosting. A turbo rebuild (cleaning or replacing the vane mechanism) is possible, but if the turbo has failed catastrophically, debris can enter the engine. Regular oil changes and using the correct oil spec are the best prevention.

problems.title: Thermostat Housing Failure
problems.symptoms: Coolant loss, overheating, low coolant warning, sweet smell from engine bay.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (unless overheating leads to head gasket damage)

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if coolant loss is detected

problems.recommendation: The thermostat housing on the 368DT is made of plastic and is prone to cracking with age and heat cycling. This can cause sudden coolant loss and overheating — a potentially catastrophic failure for any engine. If you notice coolant loss or a sweet smell, get the cooling system pressure-tested immediately. Replacing the thermostat housing proactively at 80,000 miles is cheaper than dealing with a warped head or blown head gasket.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Range Rover 3.6 TDV8 — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Straightforward replacement; catch it before turbos are affected
Labour-intensive but viable if caught early
Turbo rebuild is cost-effective if caught before catastrophic failure
Debris ingestion often means a full engine replacement is required

buyingChecks.title: Buying This Range Rover 3.6 TDV8? Check These First
buyIf: * Full service history with oil changes at 8,000–10,000 mile intervals
* Recent air pipe inspection or replacement — the most common failure point
* Exhaust manifold gaskets inspected and no blowing noise
* Cooling system in good order (recent thermostat housing, water pump, radiator)
* Turbochargers functioning correctly with no hesitation or boost issues
avoidIf: * No proof of regular oil changes — this V8 diesel is unforgiving of neglect
* Blowing/ticking noise from the engine bay — exhaust manifold gaskets likely failed
* Coolant loss with no diagnosis — thermostat housing or other cooling system issue
* Any turbo-related warning lights or boost issues — costly repairs ahead

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The 3.6 TDV8 is a landmark engine — Land Rover's first V8 diesel, smooth, torquey, and refined. It's fundamentally a robust design with a CGI block capable of 350,000 km. But age is the enemy: rubber air pipes crack, plastic thermostat housings fail, exhaust manifold gaskets blow, and turbo vane mechanisms wear. Buy on service history, budget for preventative maintenance, and this engine will reward you. Ignore the warning signs, and the bills will be eye-watering.

cta.label: → Compare Range Rover 3.6 TDV8 engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Land Rover Range Rover 3.6 TDV8 (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for ticking or blowing noise (exhaust manifold gasket warning)
* Service History — Oil changes every 8,000–10,000 miles are essential for this V8 diesel
* Boost Check — Any hissing, loss of power, or black smoke? (air pipe or turbo issues)
* Coolant Level — Check cold and hot; any loss indicates thermostat housing or cooling system issues
* Smoke Check — Black smoke = boost or fuelling issues; white smoke = coolant in cylinders
* Turbo Performance — Test acceleration; any hesitation, surging, or overboosting?
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Boost, EGR, or turbo codes are most common
* MOT History — Check for emissions failures (often related to boost or EGR issues)

items.question: What are the common problems with the Range Rover 3.6 TDV8?
items.answer: Air pipe cracking and boost leaks (the most common issue), exhaust manifold gasket failure (labour-intensive to repair), turbocharger vane mechanism wear (variable geometry turbos), and thermostat housing cracking (can cause sudden coolant loss). items.question: How do I find my Range Rover 3.6 TDV8 engine code?
items.answer: The engine code 368DT is listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The 3.6 TDV8 was fitted to L322 Range Rovers from late 2006 to 2010 and L320 Range Rover Sports from 2007 to 2010. If your vehicle is a 2006–2010 L322 or 2007–2010 L320 diesel V8, it's the 368DT (the later 4.4-litre TDV8 replaced it in 2010/2011). Check your V5C or VIN plate for confirmation.

At the first sign of boost issues or turbo noise, book an inspection immediately.
